text: The surgeon identifies the costal margin, a bony landmark along the lower edge of the rib cage, as part of the anatomical orientation during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the rib, which is part of the anatomical landmarks relevant to the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the rectus muscle, which is visible beneath the costal margin and adjacent to the rib.
The surgeon positions the fourth robotic arm, followed by the right arm equipped with scissors, preparing for precise tissue manipulation.
The surgical team positions the ports in a relatively straight line along the border of the rectus muscle, with the fourth arm slightly angled towards the iliac crest to avoid collisions.
The surgeon uses an air seal trocar for suctioning, noting that the patient's eleventh rib is depressed towards the abdomen, which obscures the view of the upper pole of the kidney and adrenal gland.
The surgeon begins dissection to locate the renal vein, carefully manipulating the descending colon, spleen, and pancreas to expose the anatomical structures.

The surgeon attempts to roll the surrounding structures away from the surgical field to expose the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal gland. The dissection proceeds to locate the renal vein, noting the presence of vascular abnormalities, including a short and wide adrenal vein with two branches leading to the adrenal gland.
The surgeon identifies a branch of the renal artery extending into the adrenal gland, preparing to clip and cut the vessel to proceed with the adrenalectomy.

The surgeon identifies congenital adhesions between the colon, spleen, and the posterior upper part of the kidney. The adhesions are carefully dissected to medialize the colon, creating a wider surgical field for better visualization.
The surgeon exposes the splenicolic ligament, revealing the spleen positioned high up in the surgical field, while ensuring wide exposure to facilitate control in case of complications.
The surgeon continues the dissection, progressing towards the spleen, carefully separating surrounding tissues to maintain a clear surgical field.
The surgical team approaches the diaphragm, upper portion of the kidney, and spleen, adjusting the position of the instruments to maintain visibility on the monitor.
The surgeon encounters difficulty maneuvering the scissors due to the rib pushing down on the instrument, affecting its movement.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon to drop it, creating space to improve visibility of the kidney hilum and renal vein. The kidney is exposed with minimal surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon identifies the colon and maintains a distance from it while separating the spleen from accessory splenic tissue.

The surgeon identifies a round structure near the spleen, possibly an extension of the spleen. A segment of Gerota's fascia is left intact to serve as a protective buttress for the delicate splenic tissue.
The pancreas is visible in the surgical field as the camera angle is adjusted. The surgeon prepares to move to another room, leaving the moderators to continue.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the end of the spleen, ensuring that the stomach does not slide down into the surgical field. An NG tube is visible, helping to prevent accidental injury to the stomach during dissection.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal mass and attempts to rotate the spleen to free it from its attachments, aiming to reduce interference from the rib as the procedure progresses medially.

The surgeon demonstrates the difference between the gyrotous fascia and the meso of the colon. The gyrotous fascia remains stationary while the meso moves, highlighting the distinct characteristics of these anatomical structures.

The surgeon identifies the mesocolon and the mass, working to mobilize the colon medially to create more space for accessing the hilum. The colon is currently obstructing the view, and efforts are focused on moving it to improve surgical access.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and notes its proximity to the hilum, while creating space for further dissection to prevent potential complications such as excessive bleeding.
The surgeon identifies the large splenic artery, which is visible and runs towards the spleen, marking the upper boundary of the dissection area.
The surgeon carefully maintains a layer of Gerota's fascia around vital structures to prevent damage, while searching for the renal vein in the surgical field.
The surgeon considers identifying the gonadal vein to follow it cranially for clearer anatomical orientation. The forearm is used as a traction device to assist in mobilizing the colon further.
The surgeon uses a forearm device to elevate and move the kidney, while employing the TIRTI-UP technique to manipulate the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully moves towards the renal hilum, aiming to locate the renal vein, while maintaining traction using the TIRTI-UP technique.

The surgeon identifies the renal vein amidst vascular abnormalities around the renal hilum and adrenal gland. The adrenal mass is visible adjacent to the renal vein, and the surgeon prepares to dissect further using a suction device.
The surgeon uses scissors to navigate the surgical field, attempting to locate the adrenal vein, which is described as having a broad and short origin before bifurcating.
The surgeon identifies a vessel that appears to be the adrenal vein, noting its unexpected length. The surgical field is adjusted to create more room for further dissection.

The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein descending and the renal vein in the surgical field. On the medial side of the adrenal gland, lymphatic tissue is visible. An assistant gently pushes down on the area to improve exposure.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, noting its length is greater than anticipated based on preoperative imaging. The surgeon assesses the possibility of maneuvering underneath the vein.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, noting adhesions along its path. Careful dissection is performed around the vein, which is typically the largest vessel in this area. A small branch of the vein is also visible.
The surgeon observes a large renal vein with no visible bifurcation, carefully inspecting the vessel for any potential branching.
The surgeon begins to separate the adrenal gland from the upper pole of the kidney, identifying a renal artery branch visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters difficulty maneuvering the scissors near the splenic artery, which is positioned close to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the upper pole of the kidney and begins to tackle the adrenal gland from this side, searching for a suitable dissection plane to follow.
The surgeon identifies the kidney and begins mobilizing the perirenal fat along with the adrenal gland, ensuring a safe distance from the mass. The fat appears sticky during manipulation.

The surgeon identifies small bleeders within the fat between the kidney and adrenal gland, using clips or cautery to manage them. The upper part of the kidney is visible, with adhesions and a potential vessel present.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein and carefully releases the mass from its proximity, using suction and preparing to place a small clip below the bipolar instrument.
The surgeon identifies the renal artery, visible in the surgical field, and examines its position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies a branch of the renal artery extending towards the adrenal gland, noting its proximity. The team discusses the availability of small or medium clips for securing the vessel.
The surgeon uses a clip-applier to secure a branch near the renal artery, followed by suction to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the complex vascular anatomy near the adrenal gland, attempting to reach the psoas muscle to confirm access to the posterior surface. The renal vein has been clipped, revealing another vessel in the area.
The surgeon reaches the surface of the kidney and continues dissection in the area, with assistance to adjust the screen for better visibility.
The surgeon mobilizes the inferior and medial aspects of the adrenal gland, preparing to isolate it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies small arteries near the adrenal gland, applying clips to prevent bleeding. The adrenal gland is carefully maneuvered to respect surrounding vascular structures, ensuring minimal disruption.

The surgeon identifies small vessels near the adrenal gland and applies a medium clip to secure them, demonstrating a strategic approach by addressing easier sections before tackling more challenging areas.

The surgeon identifies small lymphatic and vascular structures near the adrenal gland, which require careful management to prevent complications. The dissection progresses towards the psoas muscle, with the surgeon cutting through the surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon identifies lymphatic structures near the adrenal gland, noting the presence of an artery and vein extending from the Achilles side to the adrenal side. Careful consideration is given to preserving these lymphatics, although removal may be necessary.

The surgeon attempts to use the fourth arm of the robotic system but finds it obstructive. The surgical field is prepared near the pancreas, where caution is advised due to the presence of vessels. A medium-sized clip is applied to secure the area.
The surgeon identifies large vessels in the surgical field and prepares to secure them using two large clips, placing one below and one above the vessels.

The surgeon approaches from the top and carefully maneuvers downward, handling the vessels with precision. Additional handling is required due to the size of the vessels, ensuring they are adequately managed before proceeding.
The surgeon observes the lateral side of the adrenal gland, noting that it is almost clean, indicating that dissection and removal of surrounding tissue have been largely completed.
The surgeon uses a suction device to clear the surgical field, removing fluids and debris to improve visibility.
The surgeon identifies medium-sized vessels and applies a clip to secure them, followed by suctioning in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows the contours of the truss muscle, while periodically cleaning the lens to maintain clear visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel and uses cautery to prevent bleeding, ensuring no residual bleeding remains. A larger vessel is noted and addressed with careful technique.
The surgeon identifies the spleen and the splenic artery, noting minimal blood loss in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to maneuver around a sizable mass using scissors, encountering resistance that requires additional force to navigate the tissue.
The surgeon identifies a tough bleeder from the side and prepares to address it, with another team member positioned in front of the surgeon.

The surgeon identifies multiple vascular structures, including a large artery near the adrenal gland. One artery is preserved for later dissection, while another is being carefully isolated and taken.
The surgeon identifies the renal artery and renal vein, pointing them out on the screen for visualization.

The surgeon identifies the healthy part of the adrenal gland, considering the possibility of a partial adrenalectomy. The spleen is lifted slightly to improve access, though the scissors are moving with difficulty due to spatial constraints.
